sentences.text: Yes.
The ride took eight days.
And date, June 8, 1942.
I arrived at Germany.
And the people, when we arrived there--
First of all, I would like to describe a little bit-- the people in the train were talking to each other.
They knew each other.
They were sharing things with each other.
I was sitting isolated in the corner.
And I figured, I don't want to take off my [INAUDIBLE].
I don't want to take-- because I'm all alone.
They'll ask me if there are some other people from-- other girls from my place.
So when we came to Przemysl, the train stopped.
They ordered us out of the train.
We went to take showers, to clean.
They gave us certain foods.
And then I met there some Jewish people walking in the street.
Because we were all in the ghetto at that time.
And they were talking a lot as if they would have been in the train going somewhere, not to be in the ghetto.
And my heart was aching.
I saw them with the Jewish Stars on.
Now, after we went back on the train, we arrived in [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE], there was already lined up people who came to greet us, to take us to work.
And there came farmers, there came other people.
And then I started-- one of the guys, [INAUDIBLE].
He was the first one who came to us, I guess from the Gestapo.
And then I was thinking, where shall I register to go to work?
Going to a farm, I didn't have no knowledge and experience how to milk cows, how to work in a farm.
I said, they'll recognize immediately that I am not the Christian girl.
I can't do this.
Christian girls always, they having the cows.
They know how to be with the-- So I registered.
I said to them, I would like to work in a hospital somewhere.
And also I did a mistake by that time.
I said, if they ask if somebody knows a little German, that I just-- because to help us to register the people.
I volunteered.
And I said, I know some German.
This I did a very big mistake.
Because immediately, some other people got jealous of me.
Because they figured if I said I know German, to register them, they give me a better job, also give me more things than they have.
This was a mistake.
But all right.
I went with a group of girls.
There was some nuns came out with their blue uniforms, [NON-ENGLISH] nuns.
And they took us with them.
And as I recall, I went to the [INAUDIBLE] to work.
And there was several nuns.
It was like here, a holdings home, a place for soldiers recuperating from the war.
And they try to teach me what I have to do.
They gave me a list of things.
And I was very comfortable with them.
I didn't feel no prejudice.
They didn't know my identification, nothing.
And I told them I'm [NON-ENGLISH].
And I was with them for quite a long time.
But then [INAUDIBLE] used to come very often.
I don't know.
I've been thinking somebody from the group told them that something is suspicious about me.
Speaking German, well, German I studied in school a little, number one.
Number two, certain words like Jewish.
The pronunciation is a little difficult, but the basic words are-- well, I spoke German, I suppose with a little Jewish accent.
It's could be.
The girls-- now I was eight girls.
We were in a room.
And they were speaking-- they were all from one village.
They knew each other.
They were speaking Ukrainian among them.
And I was working with them.
And I saw the way they were very Catholic, religious.
And I tried to do the same way, imitated the same way as they were doing.
But they were suspicious in a way, very suspicious.
Sometimes I was sleeping.
And I had dreams.
And I was talking out loud in my dream, [INAUDIBLE] in Jewish, and say something.
They asked me many questions.
They asked me-- we all were getting letters from Ukraine.
How about you?
Where's your mail?
And I had to tell them all kind of lies.
I had a step mother.
She didn't like me, and this and that.
I had to tell them something.
And [INAUDIBLE] used to come very often to check on us.
As a matter of fact, once he came in and looked in my drawers.
Somebody must have told him about me something.
He looked in the drawers, from my small dresser, to find out if something is there.
But then after that, after that they send me away to work.
The [NON-ENGLISH] barrack.
It was for [NON-ENGLISH] barrack.
So at that time I was dealing only with people, sick people from the east.
[NON-ENGLISH].
I think that must have been also somebody that told their [INAUDIBLE].
Because he called me into his office.
And he said to me, Marie, [NON-ENGLISH].
What nationality [NON-ENGLISH].
And I looked in his eyes.
And I'm saying I'm [NON-ENGLISH].
And I'm Ukrainian.
And he looked at me.
And he looked at me.
And I didn't even wink with my eyes.
And after that, he send me away to the [NON-ENGLISH] with the [NON-ENGLISH] barrack.
And I was there with a medic, Dr.
[INAUDIBLE].
He was in charge of the barrack.
This wasn't so good for me, because again, I was in touch with the Ukrainian boys and girls.
And they tried to question my identification, my ID.
All kind-- cleaning work, taking care of sick people.
There were many sick people used to came to the barrack.
And they didn't give them too much medications.
If the person is sick, it's not worth it to live.
They can't do nothing with them.
Many people died prematurely.
There was people with VD, syphilis.
There was people, some of them were-- one person was-- I remember he was jumping on the table.
He must have been very bad temperature, 103, something, very high temperature, and he was jumping.
It was-- the people there, I remember, they didn't get too much medication.
And if the person is sick, they let them finish up.
They let them die.
They didn't-- and I of course, I had to help these people.
I had to bring the food.
The food we brought from the main office, from the main hospital, to the [NON-ENGLISH] barracks.
And that was going on also for quite a few-- for a long time, till 1943, I suppose.
So they didn't really provide medical care, because these were the guest arbeiters, the gastarbeiters from the east?
Yes.
Yeah.
You want to talk about the soldiers?
Well, the soldiers was the regular care, regular hospitals with linen, white linen, change the linen every day, and so forth.
They had very good care, the soldiers who came from the front.
The first place, yeah.
The first place wasn't bad.
And they taught me.
And they gave me, sometimes when it came a holiday, they gave me present , a little thing.
It wasn't bad.
As a matter of fact, and one of the nuns, she was very upset by taking me away from them.
But it must have been gone.
The room was from the people to [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE] did all the changing.
The nuns [NON-ENGLISH] nuns.
They were wearing navy blue uniforms with white hats.
And one of them, [NON-ENGLISH] Marta was very good.
As a matter of fact, I was thinking how good she is, like it was unbelievable to think she is German.
And she first talked to me.
Marie, you'll get over this.
You'll be free.
So sometimes you'll have a life for yourself.
She was a very nice, nice person, really nice person.
And sometimes she took some pictures.
I have a few pictures that she took for me.
And that's Sunday after work.
And she went with us shopping to a store.
We bought some used things, you know, the used things that they brought from the east, clothing and things like that.
So she took us, took the group of us to a store.
We should shop a little.
And once she took me-- when we went with them to church, this was on [NON-ENGLISH] Christmas.
I went with the [NON-ENGLISH] Marta to church.
And after she gave me, after we came back, she gave me a little gift, like a Christmas present.
She knitted gloves for me.
She gave it to me.
She was exceptionally good, from all the nuns.
They all-- they weren't bad.
But she was exceptional.
No.
We didn't talk politics with them.
We didn't talk politics.
We talk only what is important-- cleanliness, about things how to take care on the house, or how to take-- to work in the kitchen, everything what they taught to us-- not about the war, no.
Maybe she did.
Maybe she figured it out, because it cannot be otherwise.
She said-- because she hinted.
She said something, you'll survive, you'll go sometimes, you'll go have a better life.
That's what-- Sister Marta.
[NON-ENGLISH] barracks, yes.
And then after again, there was rumors that always, always everybody talked about me.
Everybody said something.
I don't know.
Maybe they were jealous that I spoke German with them.
They were thinking I'm talking against them or something.
They must have been.
So [INAUDIBLE] called me into the office, and said to me, what kind of a religion I am.
And I figured, this is the end of it.
After that, he send me-- not too long after that, he send me to work in the [NON-ENGLISH] in the factory.
This was the worst place of my work since I was in Germany.
It was very-- I mean, we had to work night shift.
And they-- we were afraid it was [NON-ENGLISH] the airplanes would bombard the Germany like right and left.
And we were afraid the factory is going to be bombarded.
It was a very dangerous life, the year in the factory, what I worked.
In the factory, I was in 1944, I suppose.
Yeah.
Because then after the factory was [NON-ENGLISH].
They threw some bombs.
And the factory was in flame.
And I run away.
This was the end of the factory.
In the factory I was working-- workers from many countries, there was French prisoners of war.
It was Ukrainian and Russian people working there.
There was Belgian-- from many nationalities were working there.
As a matter of fact, one fellow there, he was a-- [INAUDIBLE].
He was from France.
And he helped me, something, to lift the coils, the heavy coils, high coils, to lift them on the stand, to put them on the stand-- they were very heavy-- and leave them.
And he was talking a little more politics.
He had a secret radio from somewhere he got.
So I got a little more, more or less informed.
I was a little more informed what is going on in the war project.
The work.
The labor-- the worst was in the factory, very, very heavy labor.
And then I was afraid for the bombardment.
Day and night, day and night, the airplanes were flying and dropping bombs.
The big cities were demolished, many of the big cities.
But [INAUDIBLE], it was in flames too.
And after the factory was demolished, they brought us up with twine, because they have to pull us out from there.
Yes.
That's right.
After the factory, I run again.
I was again homeless.
And I run to Huffelsheim.
Also I didn't ask questions.
I run.
Which way to take-- this?
Shall I go to the right, or shall I go to the left side?
I didn't know which way to run.
No.
I was by myself.
I was isolated, yeah.
And then when I came to Huffelsheim, I went in to work for a farmer and his wife-- [INAUDIBLE]-- his wife.
And he taught me how to milk the cows and how to do it.
And many times he slapped me, because I didn't-- the milk didn't go to the bucket.
It's run out.
And I spilled the milk.
I spilled it, the milk.
No.
Of course not.
Of course not.
She was OK.
But he was a very strict person.
He wants me-- accurate I should do it.
But how should I know how to milk a cow?
But I was liberated by them.
While I was at their house, the Americans came in 1945.
I was all winter by them, the whole winter, yes.
I saw there was a nice house with a white fence.
And somebody was outside.
And I tapped the fence.
And I said, I've been homeless.
I was bombarded.
The factory where I worked is demolished.
And please, can I have a little job?
I will do anything, just to have a place there to sleep over.
And she was kind.
And she said, Mrs.
[INAUDIBLE], and she said, come.
We could use you.
And she says that a few cows-- and they had a saloon in the back.
They had a saloon, where Sunday, the people come from the village for schnapps, for a beer, something like that.
And I was listening to the conversation.
I tried to catch up something, hear something, what's going on.
And there I was till the very end, till I was liberated from the Americans.
He abuse-- he called me Russian swine.
Because the milk-- I spilled the milk.
I couldn't milk OK.
He did.
He did slap me sometimes.
But she was on my side.
She told-- she was yelling, and I mean, not supposed to do it.
But the last few nights they were-- the last few days and nights they were good to me.
As a fact, she said to me, don't leave us now.
Spring is coming.
We need-- they had a vineyard somewhere.
So I should go with them.
And I should help them.
And then she said, stay with us.
You're a young girl.
What would you go?
The soldiers are dangerous to be with.
Stay with us.
But I told her again, I told her I have to go.
I cannot stay.
I have somewhere.
I don't know what he may have told her.
And I left.
Yes.
I remember.
The last few days, the last days, she asked me to help her to put down the things in the basement, the valuable things.
I should go with her to the basement.
And she was good to me.
She gave me a 20 reichsmark. 20.
And she said, you'll need the money on your way.
First of all, she begged me not to go.
But she said, she figured it wouldn't help.
So she gave me the $20.
And she said, now you're going there with your reward.
And she wished me luck.
That's what it is.
You mean in the village?
Well, there was many girls working the village in different places.
So of course, if the Spring was approaching, they need laborers.
They need to work.
So they wanted the girls.
So she'll remain.
But the Germans, they lifted there on top.
And they went, some of them, they gave in.
But some of them, even a few Nazis, they didn't-- they denied that they were wed.
They tried to make up for the [? time. ?]
Where will I go?
First of all, I didn't know where I went, other than in the middle of the road.
And I didn't even know where to go.
I packed a small suitcase.
I didn't have too many things with me.
And I didn't know where to go.
It's funny.
And then I'd walk past.
And I begged them to give me a lift somewhere, to a city somewhere.
And then I suppose I went first to a camp, where they assemble people.
There was many, many refugees people working.
And the Ukrainian girls, they were work-- I didn't want to go with them anymore to ghetto.
Because I was still afraid to identify myself.
I still kept my documents.
I kept them very tight.
I didn't want to tell them my past, my real identity.
And then we came from one camp to another.
It was like DP camp.
Then I was in a DP camp, displaced persons camp.
And there we worked till the Russians came and picked us up.
As a matter of fact, I was thinking that, what shall I do next?
I was happy, of course.
I was liberated.
I was free.
But what is next, the next step will be for me?
So I figure, maybe I shall go back to my native country, from where I was born, everything.
And they need teachers now, because during the war, many teachers were lost in the army.
And I'll pick up where I left.
But when I came there, I was very much disappointed, very much.
First of all, they told me, who knows what you did in Germany?
Maybe you worked against us.
Maybe you were spying against Russia.
Look.
One girl was sitting in the office.
And she had only one leg.
She said to me, see, you are a healthy girl.
Look at me.
I went through the war.
Why do you complain?
And they didn't treat me very good-- very bad.
So when I picked up, I went-- I heard in Chernivtsi, I would find some people.
And then when I left to Chernivtsi on the train, they took me, and they asked me my ID.
And I didn't have the proper passport.
And again, they took me off from the train station-- a conductor with one of the people from the KGB.
I don't know who he was.
And they took me for a questioning.
They questioned me.
They took me in-- it's like a jail, not exactly a jail, but it looks like a jail to me.
And they asked me all kinds of questions, what my real name, what is this, what is that.
I told them.
They didn't believe me.
I told them, I told them in Germany I was working as Maria [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE].
He said, what is your real name?
And I told him.
He said well, we'll let the investigator find out your native village, if you were there.
They didn't believe me what I said.
Yes.
I told them.
I told them, all my family, the whole family was killed.
And I run away by sheer luck, or by a miracle, I run away.
That's what I told them.
And they didn't buy it.
They didn't take this for an answer.
I was back in Krasnostav, and I saw-- as a matter of fact, I ventured quickly back.
I saw the Krasnostav was like a ghost town.
Nothing was there-- no houses, everything was demolished.
Perhaps the partisans were fighting with the Germans.
I don't know what it was.
But it was demolished, like nothing.
Only what you see-- a rubber, and the rocks and things in the sky above.
That's what I saw in Kazakhstan.
No buildings, not whatsoever.
I tried to go to Iablonovka to see this woman.
I figured, let me talk to her.
At least I'll feel better.
But she was in very bad condition to.
Her son was killed in the army.
And she didn't have-- and she saw me.
She-- ooh-- you still alive.
I'm very glad to see you.
But so far she didn't encourage me to stay there.
So I left Iablonovka for the last time, and I left Krasnostav.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING]
Yes, before.
From there I went-- she told me herself.
She said, go to Chernivtsi.
Maybe you find your own people.
And there is a big city.
You'll find something.
I believe she gave me the idea to go to Chernivtsi.
Then I left the last time Kazakhstan, and that was the end of it.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Yeah.
I came in Chernivtsi and I was-- Chernivtsi is a big city with stores, lots of fur stores, and nice buildings.
And you see there is life going on.
They weren't affected by the war too much.
That's what it seems to me.
And I walk in the street.
And I walked in, went into a little store, a teeny store.
And I asked them, and I told them, I said I don't have nobody.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And if they can advise me where I should go and stay-- at least to sleep over the night.
Before I went to Chernivtsi-- before I went to Chernivtsi on the trains, they took me off-- the KGB took me off from the train.
And I run away, because it happened to be in November.
November 7 was the big holiday by them.
And they were very drunk, the people there.
And I run away from them, from the make believe jail.
It's similar to a jail, when I was there.
I run away.
And in there, they questioned me, my what I did in Germany.
They gave me a very bad time.
But I run away from them.
I run away.
And from them I came to Chernivtsi.
Yes.
OK.
